boundary_setting.analytic_question: Would changing the boundary of the unit or category change the interpretation?

counterfactual_tests.question: If the interpretive unit, category scope, or speaker boundary were fixed differently, would the proposed interpretation change?
Ask whether changing the boundary of the annotation would change the result; if the boundary is stable but warrants interact, use warrant_relation.
Ask whether the relevant unit/scope is unstable, or whether the unit is stable but needs external contextual inference to support conversion.

boundary_setting.decision_consequence: The record should identify the unstable boundary and explain the chosen scope.
boundary_setting.definition: Instability over the relevant case, interpretive unit, category boundary, speaker boundary, or scope of application is dominant.
do_not_use_when: Do not use when the unit is stable and the issue is a missing contextual bridge; use context_inference.
Do not use for any segmentation note that does not affect the decision.
Do not use when warrants within a stable boundary interact; use warrant_relation.
boundary_setting.escalation_condition: Escalate when the boundary cannot be fixed without evidence outside the packet.

near_miss.text: Artificial near miss: a stable paragraph needs historical background to interpret a term. That is context_inference.

positive.text: Artificial example: a two-line notice may belong either to the speaker’s statement or to a narrator’s aside, and the interpretation changes with that boundary.

exclusion_indicators: The packet boundary is merely administrative.
The boundary question is resolved before coding and no longer affects the decision.
positive_indicators: The interpretation changes depending on whether a quoted sentence belongs to one speaker or another.
The relevant episode boundary is uncertain and affects the label.
boundary_setting.provenance_note: Definition inherits the active lineage table and protocol boundaries; operational rules are v0.1 synthesis from lineage, predicted-confusion tests, protocol schema, and legacy manual distinctions.
use_another_value_when: Use context_inference when the unit is stable but external context is needed.
Use warrant_relation when the boundary is stable and warrants interact.
Use perspective_assignment when a speaker or focalizer boundary specifically controls standpoint.
use_when: The record depends on where the case begins or ends.
A speaker, category, episode, or scope boundary is unstable.
Different reasonable boundaries would produce different interpretations.

context_inference.analytic_question: Is the local evidence stable but insufficient without a contextual bridge?

counterfactual_tests.question: If the contextual bridge beyond the local anchor were unavailable, would the proposed interpretation no longer be licensed?
Ask whether the relevant unit/scope is unstable, or whether the unit is stable but needs external contextual inference to support conversion.
Ask whether later readability is the decisive feature; if yes, use temporal_layering rather than context_inference.
Ask whether the local text contains interacting warrants; if yes, use warrant_relation. Use context_inference only when the missing contextual bridge is dominant.

context_inference.decision_consequence: The record should name the contextual bridge and mark uncertainty when it is not fully supplied by the packet.
context_inference.definition: A contextual bridge beyond the local anchor is the dominant obstacle to evidence-to-interpretation conversion.
do_not_use_when: Do not use as a residual bucket for difficulty.
Do not use when a time layer in the packet does the work; use temporal_layering.
Do not use when local warrants interact; use warrant_relation.
context_inference.escalation_condition: Escalate when the required bridge is external to the packet and cannot be responsibly supplied.

near_miss.text: Artificial near miss: the local passage contains two explicit rules that conflict. That is warrant_relation.

positive.text: Artificial example: a phrase can function as a legal release only if a recorded institutional convention is accepted as context.

exclusion_indicators: The context is available locally as a warrant relation.
The missing bridge is caused by unclear packet boundaries.
positive_indicators: A local phrase needs an institutional convention to explain its function.
A stable sentence requires genre context to justify the label.
context_inference.provenance_note: Definition inherits the active lineage table and protocol boundaries; operational rules are v0.1 synthesis from lineage, predicted-confusion tests, protocol schema, and legacy manual distinctions.
use_another_value_when: Use boundary_setting when the relevant unit or scope itself is unstable.
Use temporal_layering when later textual framing reclassifies earlier evidence.
Use warrant_relation when interacting local warrants carry the decision.
use_when: The packet evidence needs social, genre, institutional, historical, or discourse context to support the interpretation.
The local unit is stable but the needed bridge is not fully inside it.
More specific loci have been excluded.

cue_function.analytic_question: Is the unresolved conversion produced by what kind of cue this is, rather than by who uses it, how it is used, or how warrants relate?

counterfactual_tests.question: If the same local content arrived through a different cue family, would the proposed interpretation change while speaker, operation, warrant, time, boundary, and context stayed stable?
Ask whether the problem is what this cue type does here, or what an act of interpretation, consultation, confession, recognition, or self-reading does.
Ask whether the cue type itself is underdetermined after excluding more specific loci, or whether one source, sign, medium, speaker, or result is granted standing.

cue_function.decision_consequence: Use only with care; if accepted, the record says the unresolved converter is cue-family function itself.
cue_function.definition: The cue family itself remains the dominant procedural locus after more specific operation, warrant, relation, perspective, temporal, boundary, and context loci have been excluded.
do_not_use_when: Do not use when a visible interpretive act converts the evidence; use operation_function.
Do not use when a specific source or sign is granted authority; use warrant_attribution.
Do not use as a default for difficult cases.
cue_function.escalation_condition: Escalate when cue_function is proposed but another specific locus remains plausible at equal or higher confidence.

near_miss.text: Artificial near miss: a judge reads a seal aloud and declares it valid. The visible act of reading and validating is operation_function or warrant_attribution, not cue_function.

positive.text: Artificial example: a packet contains a sealed red mark whose genre is unspecified. The record cannot decide whether the mark functions as warning, authorization, or ritual token, and no speaker, operation, or warrant relation resolves it.

exclusion_indicators: A named cue appears only as topic vocabulary.
The cue is stable and the problem is authority, relation, operation, or context.
positive_indicators: A cue type is named but its procedural role remains unsettled after other checks.
Different cue-family readings would change the primary interpretation.
cue_function.provenance_note: Definition inherits the active lineage table and protocol boundaries; operational rules are v0.1 synthesis from lineage, predicted-confusion tests, protocol schema, and legacy manual distinctions.
use_another_value_when: Use operation_function when the act of reading, confessing, consulting, recognizing, or interpreting does the work.
Use warrant_attribution when the issue is whether one source, sign, or medium has standing.
Use context_inference when the local cue is stable but needs an external bridge.
use_when: The record turns on the function of a cue type such as omen, testimony, prophecy, sign, inscription, confession, or token.
No more specific locus explains the instability.
The cue family remains unstable even after evidence, speaker, operation, warrant, time, boundary, and context checks.

operation_function.analytic_question: Does an act performed in or by the record do the converting work?

counterfactual_tests.question: If the visible operation were removed but the same evidence remained, would the proposed interpretation still follow?
Ask whether the problem is what this cue type does here, or what an act of interpretation, consultation, confession, recognition, or self-reading does.
If evidence becomes usable as a warrant through the operation, prefer warrant_attribution; if evidence already has standing and the function depends on handling it, prefer operation_function.
If the operation itself performs the conversion, prefer operation_function; if it mainly relates multiple warrants, prefer warrant_relation.
If the local operation produces the function, prefer operation_function; if a later moment reclassifies earlier evidence, prefer temporal_layering.
If the function is produced by local operation, prefer operation_function; if a later moment reclassifies earlier evidence, prefer temporal_layering.
Ask whether the function depends on the act of confessing/testifying or on whose perspective/testimonial role controls the annotation.

operation_function.decision_consequence: The record should cite the operation and specify what conversion it performs.
operation_function.definition: A visible interpretive, testimonial, consultative, recognitional, or self-reading operation converts evidence into the primary interpretation.
do_not_use_when: Do not use when the operation only grants standing to one warrant; consider warrant_attribution.
Do not use when a later time layer reclassifies earlier evidence; use temporal_layering.
Do not use for any action; the action must perform interpretive conversion.
operation_function.escalation_condition: Escalate when operation and warrant attribution or temporal layering are equally plausible.

near_miss.text: Artificial near miss: the mark is accepted because it bears an official seal; that is warrant_attribution.

positive.text: Artificial example: a messenger deciphers a mark and the decision depends on that deciphering rather than on the mark alone.

exclusion_indicators: The act is physical but not interpretive.
The operation is mentioned but not needed for the decision.
positive_indicators: A character interprets a sign and that act supplies the function.
A confession changes how earlier evidence is read.
operation_function.provenance_note: Definition inherits the active lineage table and protocol boundaries; operational rules are v0.1 synthesis from lineage, predicted-confusion tests, protocol schema, and legacy manual distinctions.
use_another_value_when: Use warrant_attribution when authority assigned to a source is dominant.
Use warrant_relation when the operation primarily ranks or relates multiple warrants.
Use perspective_assignment when the speaker or standpoint controls the function.
use_when: The evidence becomes meaningful because someone interprets, recognizes, confesses, testifies, consults, translates, classifies, or self-reads.
The operation is visible in the packet and not merely inferred as background.
Changing or removing the operation would change the interpretation.

perspective_assignment.analytic_question: Would changing the speaker, witness position, or standpoint change the interpretation?

counterfactual_tests.question: If the same words or observations came from a different standpoint or witness role, would the proposed interpretation change?
Ask whether incompatibility among multiple warrants determines the function, or whether changing the speaker/testimonial position would change the annotation.
Ask whether changing the speaker/testimonial position would change the annotation; if incompatibility among multiple warrants is dominant, use warrant_relation.
Ask whether the function depends on the act of confessing/testifying or on whose perspective/testimonial role controls the annotation.

perspective_assignment.decision_consequence: The record should identify the controlling standpoint and its effect.
perspective_assignment.definition: Whose perspective, witness role, testimonial position, focalization, or narrative standpoint determines the function.
do_not_use_when: Do not use merely because a speaker is present.
Do not use when multiple accounts interact as warrants; use warrant_relation.
Do not use when a confession or testimony matters as an operation; use operation_function.
perspective_assignment.escalation_condition: Escalate when perspective control and warrant relation remain inseparable.

near_miss.text: Artificial near miss: two witnesses disagree and the record ranks their accounts. That is warrant_relation.

positive.text: Artificial example: a line appears certain when read as narrator report but doubtful when read as a frightened witness’s memory.

exclusion_indicators: Speaker identity is stable and not interpretively consequential.
The perspective is only one warrant among multiple ranked warrants.
positive_indicators: The same statement functions differently because it is internal focalization rather than public report.
A witness role changes whether evidence counts as observation or rumor.
perspective_assignment.provenance_note: Definition inherits the active lineage table and protocol boundaries; operational rules are v0.1 synthesis from lineage, predicted-confusion tests, protocol schema, and legacy manual distinctions.
use_another_value_when: Use warrant_relation when the issue is conflict among several accounts.
Use operation_function when the act of confessing, testifying, or recognizing performs conversion.
Use warrant_attribution when one speaker is simply granted authority.
use_when: The function depends on who sees, speaks, reports, remembers, or frames the evidence.
A witness role or focalizer controls the force of the record.
Changing standpoint would change the primary interpretation.

temporal_layering.analytic_question: Does a later or retrospective layer reclassify what earlier evidence does?

counterfactual_tests.question: If the later or retrospective layer were removed, would the earlier evidence keep the same function?
If removing cross-warrant conflict removes the function, prefer warrant_relation; if removing the later temporal frame removes it, prefer temporal_layering.
If the local operation produces the function, prefer operation_function; if a later moment reclassifies earlier evidence, prefer temporal_layering.
If removing the later temporal frame removes the function, prefer temporal_layering; if removing cross-warrant conflict removes it, prefer warrant_relation.
If the function is produced by local operation, prefer operation_function; if a later moment reclassifies earlier evidence, prefer temporal_layering.
Ask whether later readability is the decisive feature; if yes, use temporal_layering rather than context_inference.

temporal_layering.decision_consequence: The record should name the earlier evidence and the later layer that reclassifies it.
temporal_layering.definition: Later fulfilment, retrospective framing, historical readability, or reuse across time changes the function of earlier evidence.
do_not_use_when: Do not use merely because events happen in sequence.
Do not use when a local recognition act performs the conversion; use operation_function.
Do not use when multiple warrants conflict without time-layered reclassification; use warrant_relation.
temporal_layering.escalation_condition: Escalate when the record cannot decide whether time layering or local operation is dominant.

near_miss.text: Artificial near miss: two simultaneous statements conflict. That is warrant_relation.

positive.text: Artificial example: a harmless greeting becomes evidence of threat only after a later reply reveals prior knowledge.

exclusion_indicators: Chronology is present but not interpretively active.
A later fact is just another warrant in a conflict.
positive_indicators: An early promise becomes evidence of irony only after a later reversal.
A later fulfilment recasts an earlier sign.
temporal_layering.provenance_note: Definition inherits the active lineage table and protocol boundaries; operational rules are v0.1 synthesis from lineage, predicted-confusion tests, protocol schema, and legacy manual distinctions.
use_another_value_when: Use operation_function when a visible local act converts evidence.
Use warrant_relation when conflict among warrants is dominant.
Use context_inference when historical background is external rather than a textual time layer.
use_when: A later event changes how earlier evidence functions.
The record depends on retrospective framing or later readability.
The same evidence has one local role and another later role.

warrant_attribution.analytic_question: Does the decision turn on whether one item is allowed to count as a warrant?

counterfactual_tests.question: If this single source, speaker, medium, sign, or result lost warranting standing, would the proposed interpretation lose its support?
Ask whether the cue type itself is underdetermined after excluding more specific loci, or whether one source, sign, medium, speaker, or result is granted standing.
Count the warrants: if the annotation depends on how more than one warrant interacts, use warrant_relation; if one source receives authority, use warrant_attribution.
If evidence becomes usable as a warrant through the operation, prefer warrant_attribution; if evidence already has standing and the function depends on handling it, prefer operation_function.

warrant_attribution.decision_consequence: The final record should cite the warrant-bearing item and explain why its standing matters.
warrant_attribution.definition: One source, medium, speaker, sign, or result is granted warranting force for the primary interpretation.
do_not_use_when: Do not use when multiple warrants must be ranked or reconciled; use warrant_relation.
Do not use when the visible operation itself converts evidence; use operation_function.
Do not use when standpoint controls the function; use perspective_assignment.
warrant_attribution.escalation_condition: Escalate when the record cannot decide whether one warrant has standing or multiple warrants are interacting.

near_miss.text: Artificial near miss: two witness notes contradict each other and the record depends on ranking them. That is warrant_relation.

positive.text: Artificial example: a witness note is treated as reliable enough to identify the speaker’s intention, and the interpretation would collapse if the note were not granted standing.

exclusion_indicators: Several sources interact and none singly carries the decision.
The source’s authority is already stable and the remaining question is how it is handled.
positive_indicators: One sign is treated as decisive evidence.
One speaker’s report is granted standing over available alternatives.
warrant_attribution.provenance_note: Definition inherits the active lineage table and protocol boundaries; operational rules are v0.1 synthesis from lineage, predicted-confusion tests, protocol schema, and legacy manual distinctions.
use_another_value_when: Use warrant_relation when the dominant instability is conflict or ranking among two or more warrants.
Use operation_function when a reading, confession, consultation, recognition, or self-reading performs the conversion.
Use perspective_assignment when changing the speaker or witness role changes the interpretation.
use_when: A single source, speaker, medium, sign, result, or textual item receives authority.
Removing that item’s standing would change the interpretation.
The problem is attribution of warranting force, not interaction among multiple warrants.

warrant_relation.analytic_question: Does the interpretation depend on how two or more warrants interact?

counterfactual_tests.question: If the same warrants no longer conflicted, ranked, qualified, or interacted, would the primary interpretation change?
Count the warrants: if the annotation depends on how more than one warrant interacts, use warrant_relation; if one source receives authority, use warrant_attribution.
If removing cross-warrant conflict removes the function, prefer warrant_relation; if removing the later temporal frame removes it, prefer temporal_layering.
Ask whether incompatibility among multiple warrants determines the function, or whether changing the speaker/testimonial position would change the annotation.
If the operation itself performs the conversion, prefer operation_function; if it mainly relates multiple warrants, prefer warrant_relation.
Ask whether changing the boundary of the annotation would change the result; if the boundary is stable but warrants interact, use warrant_relation.
If removing the later temporal frame removes the function, prefer temporal_layering; if removing cross-warrant conflict removes it, prefer warrant_relation.
Ask whether changing the speaker/testimonial position would change the annotation; if incompatibility among multiple warrants is dominant, use warrant_relation.
Ask whether the local text contains interacting warrants; if yes, use warrant_relation. Use context_inference only when the missing contextual bridge is dominant.

warrant_relation.decision_consequence: The record should name the warrants and the relation among them.
warrant_relation.definition: Conflict, ranking, qualification, suspension, or interaction among multiple warrants is the dominant procedural locus.
do_not_use_when: Do not use merely because records disagree.
Do not use when one source alone is granted standing; use warrant_attribution.
Do not use when temporal reclassification is dominant; use temporal_layering.
warrant_relation.escalation_condition: Escalate when the record cannot decide whether warrant interaction or perspective, time, operation, or boundary is dominant.

near_miss.text: Artificial near miss: a single order is simply treated as authoritative. That is warrant_attribution.

positive.text: Artificial example: one note promises release while a later order cancels it; the interpretation turns on the relation between promise and cancellation.

exclusion_indicators: The relation is incidental and another locus performs conversion.
positive_indicators: Two reports conflict and the record depends on which is prioritized.
A sign is qualified by a later statement within the same packet.
warrant_relation.provenance_note: Definition inherits the active lineage table and protocol boundaries; operational rules are v0.1 synthesis from lineage, predicted-confusion tests, protocol schema, and legacy manual distinctions.
use_another_value_when: Use warrant_attribution for one source receiving standing.
Use temporal_layering when later framing changes earlier evidence.
Use perspective_assignment when standpoint rather than cross-warrant relation determines the function.
use_when: Multiple warrants point in different or mutually qualifying directions.
The record ranks, reconciles, suspends, or combines warrants.
Removing the relation among warrants would change the interpretation.

claim_boundary: Provisional audit vocabulary for protocol review; not empirical validation, an exhaustive ontology, or a truth standard.

counterfactual_test_protocol.conflict_policy: Conflicting tests trigger escalation or unresolved; do not force a dominant locus.
mandatory_when: the coder marks medium or high uncertainty

nodes.question: Are selected evidence and focal interpretive decision recorded?
Does an unstable unit, speaker boundary, category boundary, or scope boundary control the decision?
Does a later, retrospective, or reuse layer reclassify earlier evidence?
Does standpoint, focalization, witness role, or testimonial position control the function?
Does a visible interpretive, testimonial, consultative, recognitional, or self-reading operation perform the conversion?
Does one source, medium, speaker, sign, or result receive warranting force?
Do multiple warrants have to be ranked, reconciled, qualified, suspended, or related?
Do multiple local warrants interact as the dominant problem?
Is the cue family itself still the unresolved converter after more specific loci are excluded?
Is a contextual bridge beyond the local anchor required after more specific loci are excluded?

evidence_before_locus_rule: Record evidence and rationale before assigning friction_locus; friction_locus is not a substitute for the primary label.
global_coding_sequence: identify where conversion from evidence to interpretation becomes unstable

one_dominant_locus_rule.secondary_loci: record separately in rationale/decision path only if schema permits; do not encode as multiple primary labels

pairwise_disambiguation.error_risk: Reserved cue_function may be overused when operation_function is the more specific locus.
pairwise_disambiguation.prior_examples: operation_function has prior positive examples; cue_function has no positive demo example
pairwise_disambiguation.source_of_confusion: A cue type such as prophecy, testimony, or divination may be mistaken for the visible act performed with it.
pairwise_disambiguation.test: Ask whether the problem is what this cue type does here, or what an act of interpretation, consultation, confession, recognition, or self-reading does.

pairwise_disambiguation.error_risk: Reserved cue_function may be overused when warrant_attribution is the more specific locus.
pairwise_disambiguation.prior_examples: warrant_attribution has prior positive examples; cue_function has no positive demo example
pairwise_disambiguation.source_of_confusion: A cue family may be confused with the source or medium receiving warranting force.
pairwise_disambiguation.test: Ask whether the cue type itself is underdetermined after excluding more specific loci, or whether one source, sign, medium, speaker, or result is granted standing.

pairwise_disambiguation.error_risk: One-warrant cases may be overcoded as warrant_relation when contextual complexity is present.
pairwise_disambiguation.source_of_confusion: A single source granted authority may be confused with an interaction among multiple warrants.
pairwise_disambiguation.test: Count the warrants: if the annotation depends on how more than one warrant interacts, use warrant_relation; if one source receives authority, use warrant_attribution.

pairwise_disambiguation.error_risk: Warrant attribution may be overcoded as operation_function when interpretation is visibly present.
pairwise_disambiguation.source_of_confusion: Authority is often assigned through an interpretive act, so granting standing may be confused with visible operation.
pairwise_disambiguation.test: If evidence becomes usable as a warrant through the operation, prefer warrant_attribution; if evidence already has standing and the function depends on handling it, prefer operation_function.

pairwise_disambiguation.error_risk: Warrant conflicts in retrospectively framed cases may be mistaken for temporal_layering, or temporal effects may be flattened into warrant_relation.
pairwise_disambiguation.source_of_confusion: Cross-warrant conflict may coexist with retrospective framing or later readability.
pairwise_disambiguation.test: If removing cross-warrant conflict removes the function, prefer warrant_relation; if removing the later temporal frame removes it, prefer temporal_layering.

pairwise_disambiguation.error_risk: Perspective-governed testimony may be overcoded as warrant_relation when multiple accounts are visible.
pairwise_disambiguation.source_of_confusion: Incompatible testimony or standpoint can look like conflict among warrants or like perspective control.
pairwise_disambiguation.test: Ask whether incompatibility among multiple warrants determines the function, or whether changing the speaker/testimonial position would change the annotation.

pairwise_disambiguation.error_risk: Operations that rank warrants may be overcoded as operation_function when warrant_relation is dominant.
pairwise_disambiguation.source_of_confusion: An operation can matter because it performs conversion or because it creates, exposes, ranks, or intensifies relations among warrants.
pairwise_disambiguation.test: If the operation itself performs the conversion, prefer operation_function; if it mainly relates multiple warrants, prefer warrant_relation.

pairwise_disambiguation.error_risk: Later reclassification may be overcoded as operation_function when a recognition scene is visible.
pairwise_disambiguation.source_of_confusion: Recognition or fulfilment can be treated as a local operation or as later reclassification of earlier evidence.
pairwise_disambiguation.test: If the local operation produces the function, prefer operation_function; if a later moment reclassifies earlier evidence, prefer temporal_layering.

pairwise_disambiguation.error_risk: Boundary_setting may be underused when coders move directly to warrant_relation inside an unstable unit.
pairwise_disambiguation.prior_examples: warrant_relation has prior positive examples; boundary_setting has no positive demo example
pairwise_disambiguation.source_of_confusion: A scope or unit boundary may be confused with the relation among warrants inside a stable unit.
pairwise_disambiguation.test: Ask whether changing the boundary of the annotation would change the result; if the boundary is stable but warrants interact, use warrant_relation.

pairwise_disambiguation.error_risk: Boundary problems may be absorbed into context_inference as a residual explanation.
pairwise_disambiguation.prior_examples: neither boundary_setting nor context_inference has a positive demo example in the cited manual lineage
pairwise_disambiguation.source_of_confusion: A missing boundary may be confused with a missing contextual bridge.
pairwise_disambiguation.test: Ask whether the relevant unit/scope is unstable, or whether the unit is stable but needs external contextual inference to support conversion.

pairwise_disambiguation.error_risk: Temporal_layering may be undercoded when later readability appears alongside multiple warrants.
pairwise_disambiguation.source_of_confusion: Later fulfilment, retrospective readability, or reuse may coexist with local warrant conflict.
pairwise_disambiguation.test: If removing the later temporal frame removes the function, prefer temporal_layering; if removing cross-warrant conflict removes it, prefer warrant_relation.

pairwise_disambiguation.error_risk: Temporal effects may be overcoded as operation_function when the text includes a visible recognition act.
pairwise_disambiguation.source_of_confusion: A visible recognition or fulfilment scene can look like a local operation.
pairwise_disambiguation.test: If the function is produced by local operation, prefer operation_function; if a later moment reclassifies earlier evidence, prefer temporal_layering.

pairwise_disambiguation.error_risk: Perspective_assignment may be undercoded when multiple accounts make warrant conflict salient.
pairwise_disambiguation.source_of_confusion: Perspective, witness role, or testimonial position may be confused with incompatibility among multiple accounts as warrants.
pairwise_disambiguation.test: Ask whether changing the speaker/testimonial position would change the annotation; if incompatibility among multiple warrants is dominant, use warrant_relation.

pairwise_disambiguation.error_risk: Perspective-governed testimony may be overcoded as operation_function when the speech act is prominent.
pairwise_disambiguation.source_of_confusion: Confession or testimony can be treated as a visible operation or as standpoint-governed perspective.
pairwise_disambiguation.test: Ask whether the function depends on the act of confessing/testifying or on whose perspective/testimonial role controls the annotation.

pairwise_disambiguation.error_risk: Context_inference may be overused when temporal_layering provides the more specific explanation.
pairwise_disambiguation.prior_examples: temporal_layering has prior positive examples; context_inference has no positive demo example
pairwise_disambiguation.source_of_confusion: Historical readability or later framing may be mistaken for external contextual inference.
pairwise_disambiguation.test: Ask whether later readability is the decisive feature; if yes, use temporal_layering rather than context_inference.

pairwise_disambiguation.error_risk: Context_inference may be overused as a residual category for difficult warrant interactions.
pairwise_disambiguation.prior_examples: warrant_relation has prior positive examples; context_inference has no positive demo example
pairwise_disambiguation.source_of_confusion: External or background context may be confused with interaction among warrants within the text.
pairwise_disambiguation.test: Ask whether the local text contains interacting warrants; if yes, use warrant_relation. Use context_inference only when the missing contextual bridge is dominant.

unresolved_policy.use_when: Evidence and counterfactual tests do not support a responsible dominant locus.
